entries.what_they_read: I saw 'Trade player tokens that move with real stats' up top, then the three-step breakdown — get $100 fake money, find a mispriced player, buy or sell based on whether you think stats catch up. Also caught the 'AWAITING TIP-OFF' banner and the LeBron example with the $5.59 vs $4.20 gap.
entries.their_interpretation: So this is basically like a fantasy stock market for NBA players — no real cash involved, just play money, and the whole game is spotting when the crowd has a player priced wrong versus what his actual stats say he's worth. Feels like if ESPN fantasy and a DEX had a baby but kept it simple.
entries.their_rationale: No seed phrase, no signup, no real money on the line — that's the trust unlock for me. The vibe feels clean and the LeBron gap example actually made sense in basketball terms, not just crypto terms, so I'm not feeling like I'm about to get rugged on some janky token site.
confusion_notes: 'Fair-value price' — I get the concept but I want to know exactly how the computer calculates it from stats, like is it PER, usage rate, what?
'The market decides what it actually trades at' — who IS the market here if everyone's on fake money? Are there bots? Is it other real users?
'Your $100 only nudges a star like LeBron' — so my trades literally move prices? That feels gameable, not sure I fully trust that mechanic yet.
'AWAITING TIP-OFF' — cool flavor text but I wasn't 100% sure if that meant I literally can't trade until tonight's games start or if it's just aesthetic
first_impression_elements: Big 'Claim $100 →' button — jumped out immediately, no-risk framing sealed it
'AWAITING TIP-OFF' ticker at the top — felt live and premium, like a real trading terminal
The LeBron $5.59 vs $4.20 undervalued example — concrete, basketball-specific, made the whole concept click fast
ignored_elements: '↓Terms explained' link at the bottom — didn't even scroll to it
'💬Feedback' button — registered it exists, moved on
The 'Thesis' one-liner about being right early — saw it, felt like filler copy, didn't dwell

checkpoint.q1_what_am_i_buying: Okay so from what I can tell, you're buying like a fake share of a player — it's not real stock or anything, it's just pretend money tied to how that player performs statistically. Like if I buy a Joel Embiid token, I'm basically betting that his numbers are gonna back up what I know about him.
checkpoint.q2_what_moves_price: Two things it seems like — the stats move some kind of 'fair value' price that the computer calculates, and then other people buying or selling moves the actual market price. So if everybody's sleeping on Joel and I buy, that nudges the price up a little for the next person.
checkpoint.q3_hold_week: Honestly I'm not totally sure — I think the fair value keeps updating as stats come in each day, so if LeBron has a couple big games that fair value goes up? But whether the market price follows it automatically or I gotta wait for other people to catch on... I'm guessing it's the second one, which means I might just be sitting there watching. The app wasn't super clear on that.
checkpoint.q4_fair_vs_market: Fair value is what the computer says the player is worth based on real stats, and market price is what people are actually paying for the token right now. The gap between those two is supposedly where you make your money — if stats say Embiid is worth more than what the market has him at, you buy and wait for everyone else to figure it out.

first_impression_elements: That big orange 'Claim $100' button — hard to miss, jumps right out
The 'real stats' in orange on the headline — clean contrast, felt intentional
The 'AWAITING TIP-OFF' ticker at the top with the orange dot — felt live, like something's actually happening
ignored_elements: The 'How it works' secondary link — barely registered next to that CTA
The grid/graph background texture — didn't really process it
The 'Feedback' chat bubble in the corner — tuned it out completely
data.visual_notes: Dark mode with orange accents hits right for a sports-trading vibe — feels like it belongs next to ESPN or The Athletic. The layout is clean but sparse, almost too minimal for something asking me to eventually put money in; there's no product screenshot or UI preview to anchor trust. The 'no real money' disclaimer line at the bottom is small and easy to scroll past, which on mobile is a miss if they actually want me to feel safe.
